result:
The rain doesn't fall here anymore. Not really. It arrives as a soft, silvery mist that clings to everything like forgotten memories. I watch it from my window each morning, the way it turns the neon signs into bleeding watercolors against the glass towers. 

This city never sleeps, but it dreams in fragments. Last night I followed a stray cat through the underground markets where they sell dreams by the gram—little glass vials of other people's happiest moments. The seller told me the blue ones were from children who still believed in magic. I didn't buy any. Some things are too expensive even when they're cheap.

My neighbor plays the same three notes on his broken piano every evening at 7:12. Never more, never less. I've come to understand this as a form of prayer. The woman across the street collects books she cannot read, stacking them like bricks against the loneliness. We wave sometimes. Our hands look like tired birds.

I think I'm becoming the kind of person who talks to vending machines. They never answer, but at least they dispense something tangible. Yesterday one gave me strawberry milk instead of coffee. I took it as a sign.

The truth is, we're all just stories trying to become real. Some of us are novels, some of us are grocery lists. I'm learning to be okay with being a footnote that somehow wandered into the main text. The mist keeps falling. The city keeps dreaming. And somewhere between the neon and the silence, I exist—imperfectly, beautifully, completely.

(Word count: 248)
